classification: TLP:AMBER — Share with sector peers and government agencies only

issuer.division: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)

title: CERT-In Advisory: Targeted Ransomware Campaign Against Healthcare and Education Sector Critical National Infrastructure
affected_sectors: Healthcare — Public Hospitals and Medical Institutions
Education — Central Examination Bodies and Universities
summary: CERT-In has identified an active, coordinated ransomware campaign targeting healthcare and education sector organizations in India. The threat actor demonstrates a sophisticated multi-stage attack methodology combining spearphishing, valid account exploitation, lateral movement via Windows administrative shares, and Cobalt Strike C2 infrastructure. The campaign includes a pre-encryption data exfiltration phase enabling double-extortion. Multiple Indian CNI organizations have been compromised. The threat actor's TTPs are consistent with a sophisticated, state-affiliated advanced persistent threat (APT) group with prior operational history against Asian government and healthcare targets.

threat_actor_profile.suspected_affiliation: Nation-state affiliated — consistent with APT41 (BARIUM, Winnti Group) documented playbook
threat_actor_profile.motivation: Dual-use: intelligence collection via data exfiltration + financial extortion via ransomware
threat_actor_profile.targeting_pattern: Spearphishing of high-value administrative accounts in public sector organizations
threat_actor_profile.operational_security: High — uses Tor exit nodes for C2, typosquat domains, Malleable C2 profiles, cleans forensic artifacts
prior_incidents: AIIMS Delhi — November 2022 (22-day dwell, ransomware deployment)

stage_1_initial_access.description: Spearphishing emails with lookalike domains targeting administrative and IT accounts. Credential harvesting via adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing proxies. Valid stolen credentials used for initial authenticated access.

stage_2_persistence.description: Scheduled tasks created with legitimate-looking names (Microsoft Edge Update variants). Tasks contain base64-encoded PowerShell downloading secondary payloads from C2.

stage_3_lateral_movement.description: SMB-based lateral movement using pass-the-hash with harvested NTLM credentials. Targets Domain Controllers for maximum domain-wide impact. Uses Windows administrative shares (admin$, C$, IPC$).
stage_3_lateral_movement.observed_pattern: Patient/Student management servers → Domain Controllers → File servers

stage_4_collection.description: 7-Zip archiving of targeted data with strong encryption (-mhe=on flag). Archives staged in Windows\Temp with misleading directory names. Volume Shadow Copy deletion before exfiltration.

stage_5_c2_exfiltration.description: Cobalt Strike HTTPS beacon via typosquat domains resolving to Tor exit nodes. Beacon intervals: 300 seconds ±15% jitter. Data exfiltrated via chunked HTTPS POST requests.
stage_5_c2_exfiltration.c2_infrastructure_pattern: Typosquat domain → Fast-flux DNS (TTL 60s) → Tor exit node → Attacker infrastructure

stage_6_impact.description: Custom ransomware with organization-name-branded extension (e.g., .AIIMS_LOCKED, .CBSE_LOCKED). Simultaneous admin account lockout via automated bad-password spray. Recovery environment disabled via bcdedit.

ip_addresses.description: Tor exit node used for Cobalt Strike C2 — AIIMS Delhi incident
Adversary-in-the-middle phishing proxy + C2 — CBSE incident
Backup C2 IP — seen in lateral movement stage of related campaign
Data exfiltration staging server — chunked HTTPS POST uploads

domains.description: Double-f typosquat impersonating Windows Update — AIIMS incident primary C2
Double-t + hyphen typosquat — CBSE incident C2 domain
Lookalike domain for CBSE official site — used in credential harvesting phishing campaign
Lookalike domain for AIIMS patient portal
Secondary C2 domain — seen in related regional hospital compromise

file_hashes.description: Custom ransomware — creates .CBSE_LOCKED extension variant, 892KB PE32+
Cobalt Strike reflective DLL loader — masquerading as Edge update component
Legitimate 7-Zip binary used for data staging — hash matches official 7-Zip 22.01 but context (parent: malicious scheduled task) is malicious
file_hashes.av_detections: 0/72 — legitimate binary, detect by behavior not hash
file_hashes.detection_note: Block by parent process behavior, not hash — legitimate 7z.exe is not malware itself

yara_rules.rule: rule CERT_IN_APT41_Ransomware_2024 { meta: description = "Detects APT41-linked ransomware variants targeting Indian CNI" author = "CERT-In" date = "2024-03-15" strings: $s1 = ".AIIMS_LOCKED" ascii $s2 = ".CBSE_LOCKED" ascii $s3 = "aiims_recovery@protonmail.com" ascii $s4 = "cbse_crisis@tutanota.com" ascii $pdb = "svhost" ascii nocase condition: any of them }

network_signatures.description: Cobalt Strike HTTPS beacon JA3 fingerprint seen in both AIIMS and CBSE incidents

network_signatures.value: Periodic HTTPS connections at 300s ±15% jitter to external IPs from servers that should not browse internet
network_signatures.description: Cobalt Strike beacon detection rule — statistical periodicity analysis required

mitigation_steps.details: Immediately block all listed IPs and domains at perimeter firewall and DNS resolver. Configure DNS RPZ (Response Policy Zones) for domain-based blocking. Alert on any existing connections to these endpoints.

Review all service accounts for excessive privileges. Apply principle of least privilege — backup service accounts should NOT have Domain Admin rights. Audit nested group memberships in Active Directory.

Deploy endpoint detection and response on all servers, especially Domain Controllers and file servers. Configure alerts for: (1) cmd.exe spawned by service accounts, (2) vssadmin delete commands, (3) 7z.exe with -p flag from unusual parent processes.

Deploy User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) to detect: (1) service account logons outside business hours, (2) SMB connections from non-administrative hosts, (3) outbound connections from Domain Controllers, (4) periodic beaconing patterns.

Configure Windows to require admin MFA before vssadmin operations. Monitor for vssadmin delete shadows commands. Maintain offline backups disconnected from the network — online backups are vulnerable to this threat actor.

Domain Controllers should NEVER make direct outbound internet connections. Implement strict firewall rules: no outbound connections from DC to internet. Route all DC internet access (Windows Update, NTP) through proxy with allowlisting.

Implement hardware FIDO2 security keys for all privileged accounts. SMS and app-based OTP MFA is susceptible to AiTM attacks used in this campaign. Conditional access policies: geo-block authentication from foreign IPs for government systems.
Under CERT-In directions (April 2022), all incidents involving ransomware targeting CNI must be reported to CERT-In within 6 hours of detection. Contact: incident@cert-in.org.in, Phone: 1800-11-4949
mitigation_steps.regulatory_reference: CERT-In Directions dated 28 April 2022 under Section 70B(6) of IT Act 2000

sigma_rules.title: Service Account Logon Outside Business Hours
sigma_rules.condition: account_name ends with '$' AND logon_type = 3 AND (hour < 8 OR hour > 18) AND weekday in (1,2,3,4,5) AND NOT (known_scheduled_task_ips contains source_ip)

sigma_rules.title: SMB Sweep Detection
sigma_rules.condition: dst_port = 445 AND count(distinct dst_ip) > 3 within 30 seconds from same src_ip AND NOT (src_ip in backup_servers)

sigma_rules.title: Domain Controller Outbound Internet Connection
sigma_rules.condition: src_ip in domain_controllers AND dst_ip NOT in (internal_ranges, known_microsoft_update_ips, ntp_servers) AND dst_port in (80, 443)

references: MITRE ATT&CK Group G0096 — APT41: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0096/
NCIIPC Guidelines for Critical Information Infrastructure: https://nciipc.gov.in/
